seed: tenderness vs rage; inside_vs_outside; quiet_channel; climb; constellation against rain; pixel_bleed; saturated_vs_muted; crown; guilt_forgiveness | abstract variation request 395; target batch count 500
title: Collision of Emotion
reasoning: This piece explores the tension between tenderness and rage, with a focus on how these emotions can coexist yet collide. The selected palette features strong contrasts, with blacks representing rage and whites and pale blues indicating tenderness. The intense, dark center of the composition is disrupted by lighter, softer pixels around the edges, symbolizing the battle between the harshness of rage and the gentleness of tenderness. Broken pixels throughout add imperfection, reflecting inner turmoil and the complexity of these feelings, embodying gaps and overlaps as both emotions vie for space. The layout suggests a crossing of horizontal and vertical movements, enhancing the feeling of tension in the center, while leaving just enough negative space to allow the emotions to breathe without overwhelming the viewer.

layout.name: cross_current
layout.description: Horizontal and vertical movements collide, creating a tense crossing in the middle.

composition.name: center_vs_edges
composition.idea: The center is protected or attacked by the surrounding pixels.

referenceExample.title: Rage Entering Calm
referenceExample.pattern: red jagged pixels cutting into blue horizontal bands

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
